entries.title: Diwali — Festival of Lights
entries.desc: A five-day festival marked by lights, family, and the triumph of good over evil.
entries.body:
Diwali is one of the most widely celebrated festivals in the world. It is celebrated over five days, with the main celebrations on the third night when families light thousands of small oil lamps called diyas.

Families decorate their homes, create colourful rangoli patterns, burst fireworks, and share sweets and gifts with neighbours.

Diwali holds different meanings across communities: for Hindus it marks Lord Ramas return; for Sikhs it marks the release of Guru Hargobind Ji; for Jains it celebrates the nirvana of Lord Mahavira.
entries.author: Priya Sharma

entries.title: Shabbat
entries.desc: The Jewish day of rest observed every week from Friday sundown to Saturday night.
entries.body:
Shabbat is the Jewish day of rest, observed every week from Friday evening at sundown until Saturday night.

Shabbat begins with the lighting of two candles. A special Friday night dinner follows, starting with blessings over challah bread and wine.

During Shabbat, observant Jewish people refrain from 39 categories of work. Instead, the day is spent attending synagogue, studying Torah, and resting with family.
entries.author: Daniel Cohen

entries.title: The Five Pillars of Islam
entries.desc: The five core acts of worship that form the foundation of a Muslims faith.
entries.body:
The Five Pillars of Islam are the five fundamental acts of worship every Muslim is expected to fulfil.

1. Shahada — The declaration of faith.

2. Salah — Prayer five times daily facing Mecca.

3. Zakat — Giving 2.5% of savings annually to those in need.

4. Sawm — Fasting during Ramadan from sunrise to sunset.

5. Hajj — A pilgrimage to Mecca at least once in a lifetime.
entries.author: Yusuf Al-Rashid
